Every solution has pros and cons, but I would opt to stay with the current spec text 9.6.1.2. This implies a prefix entry in net data with mDp = 1 but mSlaac=0 is not a valid OMR prefix, while mDp = 1 but mSlaac=1 is valid if the other conditions are also met.  (So @abtink 's choice)

If an operator configures a Domain Prefix and adds 1.1 devices (in the TC Commercial we would consider this as a corner case), the operator can choose between the two resulting behaviors depending on setting mSlaac = 0 / 1. Most likely in this scenario the operator is also able to configure the automatic OMR prefix configuration of BRs to either 'on' or 'off'.

If an operator configures a Domain Prefix and adds >= 1.2 devices (more likely scenario) then mSlaac could be to '1' and there would be no issue.

@shrekris-anyscale in general let's only try to expose complexity to users when it's necessary. In this section, I don't think we should introduce the idea of a "graph" at all until we get to the actual DAG API section.

For example, a more incremental flow would be like this:
- This intro says it's going to walk you through how to accomplish model composition (focus on job-to-be-done!), particularly how to compose multiple deployments containing ML models or business logic.
- Then the first section introduces having deployments call into each other (no need to call this a graph at all yet, the user doesn't need to be exposed to the complexity).
- Then the final section introduces the full call graph/DAG API.

> Could you please add a comment about why we need `virtualenv-clone` in this case? It's clear from the PR description and the previous PR, but it would be good to have it as a comment in the code for easier reference.

[`virtualenv-clone README`]() have some reasons why we shouldn't use virtualenv to relocate virtualenv:
```
Virtualenv provides a way to make virtualenv's relocatable which could then be copied as we wanted. 
However making a virtualenv relocatable this way breaks the no-site-packages isolation of the virtualenv 
as well as other aspects that come with relative paths and /usr/bin/env shebangs that may be undesirable.

Also, the .pth and .egg-link rewriting doesn't seem to work as intended. This attempts to overcome these 
issues and provide a way to easily clone an existing virtualenv.
```

Example: If we don't rewrite `.pth` file in `new_venv`, and this file directory to `old_venv  sit-package's dir`, then the package A  which in `old_venv sit-package's dir` got changed,  the package A of `new_venv` will also change accordingly.

```
def get_device() -> torch.device:
    """Gets the correct torch device to use for training.

    Assumes that `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` is set and is a
    superset of the `ray.get_gpu_ids()`.
    """
    if torch.cuda.is_available():
        # GPU IDs are assigned by Ray after you specify "use_gpu"
        # GPU `ray.get_gpu_ids()` may return ints or may return strings.
        # We should always convert to strings.
        gpu_ids = [str(id) for id in ray.get_gpu_ids()]

        if len(gpu_ids) > 0:
            # By default, there should only be one GPU ID if `use_gpu=True`.
            # If there are multiple GPUs, use the first one.
            # If using fractional GPUs, these IDs are not guaranteed
            # to be unique across different processes.
            gpu_id = gpu_ids[0]

            cuda_visible_str = os.environ.get("CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES", "")
            if cuda_visible_str and cuda_visible_str != "NoDevFiles":
                cuda_visible_list = cuda_visible_str.split(",")
                device_id = cuda_visible_list.index(gpu_id)
            else:
                raise RuntimeError(
                    "CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES set incorrectly. "
                    f"Got {cuda_visible_str}, expected to include {gpu_id}. "
                    "Did you override the `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` environment"
                    " variable? If not, please help file an issue on Github."
                )
        else:
            # If called on the driver or outside of Ray Train, return the
            # 0th device.
            device_id = 0
        device = torch.device(f"cuda:{device_id}")
    else:
        device = torch.device("cpu")

    return device
```

'isAcked' here tracks whether the read frame requires an Ack to be sent, or not. When no Ack is required, the logic is identical to the original code: it will report otPlatRadioReceiveDone() to handle the event of Rx done, if the frame was indeed destined to this device.

When an Ack is required, the handler otPlatRadioReceiveDone() is not called immediately - it is postponed. First the Ack is scheduled, and later on when the simulator sends the "Tx Done" event for this Ack then in the handler of that event it is checked whether the radio is actually in "receiving" state (if yes, in this case it means an Ack was either successfully transmitted or was attempted to be transmitted but failed). If that is the case, then the handler is called:
   otPlatRadioReceiveDone(aInstance, &sReceiveFrame, OT_ERROR_NONE);

The idea was that the sReceiveFrame of the last received frame is still sitting in the frame buffer and wasn't changed in the meantime.  (That assumption seems to hold due to the short time between frame reception and start of Ack sending, AIFS = 192 us, which is shorter than any PHY frame would be. Still a bit risky maybe.)

The reason this was done is that as soon as the higher-layer handler is called for a received frame, the OT stack may respond with any kind of new message (MLE, CoAP, ...) immediately and this may cause the (sub)MAC layer to switch from 'receive' to 'transmit' state and try doing CSMA, etc., which shouldn't be allowed because the Ack is still waiting to be transmitted.

Maybe better is we introduce a state variable "sRxWait" to track this?  So that the higher subMAC/MAC layers cannot grab the radio for transmission/CSMA when the Ack is still pending to be transmitted.

DUA support is currently a MUST for any Thread 1.3 device. This follows from the DUA tests in the 1.2 certification, which 1.3 inherits. DUA support in a BR role (acting as ND-Proxy) is required if the BR supports configuration of a Domain Prefix, and not required if the BR can't support configuration of a Domain Prefix.  We had quite some discussions on this in the past and so far DUA never got removed :) 

Indeed a Domain Prefix with P_slaac = 0 is used by 1.2 or higher devices to create a Domain Address using SLAAC. However Thread 1.1 devices are left in the dark if P_slaac = 0 (they can't use SLAAC then), so this could be a reason for a BR to still create a new OMR prefix. On the other hand one could say that if a Domain Prefix is configured, the admin would have also configured another prefix manually to for use by 1.1 devices if any. So I tend to just simply let the BR assume that if P_dp = 1, it implies SLAAC is possible using that prefix regardless of P_slaac's value.

To clarify, @rakanalh found a corner case that requires the blockchain query.

We must query the `locksroot` from the blockchain on the channel settled event, the reason being that we don't know if our latest message was received by the target or not, and this can happen:

- A sends a Locked transfer to B
- The lock expires
- B goes offline
- A sends to B the RemovedExpiredLock (From A's perspective we don't have any pending locks)
- The channel is closed by A
- B's monitoring service uses the Locked transfer balance proof (The blockchain state is not the same as our local state)

From A's perspective, the channel does not have any pending locks in the merkle tree, which is not true, so to avoid wrongly clearing the channel from the local state the locksroot is necessary.

To avoid `hidden` ways of fetching `zephyr.base` then we always write it during `west init`.
That means, a user can always fetch the value using `west config zephyr.base`, and each west managed project has it's own value.

If a user wants to change `zephyr.base`, as example for some local testing, he can then overwrite `zephyr.base` using environment setting, or do `west config zephyr.base <alternative_zephyr>`

As `zephyr.base` is now added during `west init` and must always be present, then the check you refer to is no longer needed.

Unfortunately, there are `west` managed projects that is not having this setting in their config file.
Thus, having the test in the `selfupdate` part is to ensure, that when user update to this `west` revision (or never) then this setting will be added to the conf file.

I think the logic here is still slightly off, as it assumes that any asset upstream of an asset produced by this step is "external".

Take for example a multi-asset that has the following internal dependency structure:

```
    a
  /   \
 b     c
```

and let's imagine "`a`" depends on "`a'`" (produced by a different step).

When we run this function to materialize a, b, and c, upstream_assets_for_asset("b")/upstream_assets_for_asset("c") will both return AssetKey("a"). This means that self._input_asset_records[AssetKey("a")] will be populated when this function is called, and so it'll have an old version, rather than the version created when running this step.

however, if we run this function to materialize just b and c, then the behavior described above is actually correct, as you don't expect to get a new version of a, so you should query for whatever the old version was.

the asset_info returned by asset_info_for_output has an "is_required" property, which will tell you if, for the current execution, that asset key is required to be executed. (generally, if is_required is `False`, then we do not expect it to be generated, although we'll just emit a warning rather than an error if it is.

I think this information should be used to get a set of required_output_keys (rather than all output keys), but mostly I'm just advocating for tests of these sorts of scenarios

Basically, because this was supposed to be a tiny, very scoped PR :-).

But code-wise, it would make sense to re-think the whole flow of information. Issues #244, #143, even #87 are related to this.
The way things are being done now, everything gets passed around all the time, which is why the public interface accepts anything and never raises an Exception.

There is the `treq.client.HttpClient.request` method, which is the one that does actually does something, everything else just modifies some arguments to it. I think it makes sense, because that way adding some feature or fixing something is done in just that spot.

But that means that wherever there is a public API (e.g. `treq.get`), there is a `_client` call, which expects things like agent, reactor, ... and then that gets passed further to `HttpClient.request`.
So there were/are two options: change the signature of all those public methods to be complete, with the downside that further improving treq becomes burdensome (change a bunch of signatures as opposed to just one), or, what is being done here, which is consume the `_client` specific arguments and don't pass them further down the line.

In any case, as @glyph mentioned the right thing is to first raise a warning for some versions, so I guess this PR will have to stay open until then.

So the LambdaMART (implicit) cost function has the following form:

log(1 + exp(-sigmoid_ * (s_i - s_j)))

The first derivative of the above has the sigmoid_ term -- so mathematically p_lambda in the code must have the sigmoid_ term in its computation. The second derivative will have sigmoid_^2 as proposed in the code change.

For more details, please see Section 7 of this paper: http://citeseerx.ist.psu.edu/viewdoc/download?doi=10.1.1.180.634&rep=rep1&type=pdf

Unrelated, this syntax is redundant: `call(**dict(key=value))`

The `**` operator unpacks a dictionary into named parameters. `dict()` builds and returns a dictionary using the provided named parameters.
Doing these in sequence in the same place is a no-op and just complicates the code.

ie, these are equivalent:
```python
aws_encryption_sdk.KMSMasterKeyProvider(**dict(key_ids=[key_arn1]))
aws_encryption_sdk.KMSMasterKeyProvider(key_ids=[key_arn1])
```

It's not only in corner of the image, but the background intensity in that area causes funny results. Inversion + subtraction with `uint` types caught me off guard as well, which is why I added this demonstration to the examples. 

The underflow occurs for every pixel except for those where the background intensity exactly matches the intensity of the image. For those pixels, the intensity will be `0` as opposed to `256` which can't be represented. As a result `image - background` removes the background AND inverts the image except for pixels where background and image intensity match. These will be `0` causing black spots that should really be 255. A "hacked" fix ala `img[img==0]=255`, won't work here either, because 0 background pixels are now aliased with pixels that *should* have value 0 (intense features).

> Also, don't you think the formulation above (image_inverted - background_inverted) is more intuitive

I agree. This is why I do this in the example above, but point out this alternative in the "gotcha" section. The idea of not duplicating that is to show how one could explicitly handle this underflow ... maybe I'm making this too complicated?

As mentioned, this is not the same as `decode_len`.

`decode_len` is a number of containers to be used in the call to `unpack`. Because the data was originally serialized as a number of containers, the calculation for `decode_len` should never have a remainder. (The Windows-specific assert checks this; I could add a more general assert too.)

In contrast, the `buflen` is a buffer we're creating now, and the size is based on the number of significant bits in the incoming data. That may not be a multiple of our container size (`KEY_BITS`), which is why we have to check the modulus here and maybe increase the size.

The net effect is what we want - lifetime is defined solely by this process (as before this change), but the subprocess lives until this process decides to shutdown.

My idea is that as soon as we configured as an eligible BR:
- If number of active routers is below threshold, we don't wait for normal mechanism to upgrade to a router role and request it immediately (this gives more chance for BRs to start as router from beginning).
  - We can still request it with `kBorderRouterRequst` reason and leader is expected to treat as a normal request when we are below the active routers threshold.
- If number of active is above threshold, then we would count the number of existing BRs (from network data) and decide based on that whether to attempt to become router or not.

I understand it going somewhat beyond what we described in the JIRA (though it still using the new mechanism) but I feel this may be useful behavior.

Thoughts?

> I think the more important consideration here is whether we want to apply some random delay. If there are a large number of BRs in the network, I could see a large number of requests being sent to the Leader at the same time whenever the network data is updated and the conditions to upgrade are satisfied.

On adding some delay to send the the requests, I think that make sense. We may be able to re-use the timer in this module (probably need to maintain some sort of state). Will try to add this later in next push.

Correct. To start, the [docs for `Borrow`][b] have this as an example
case, which I found helpful.

Probably the most foolproof way to see what’s happening is to look at
the type signature for [`HashMap<K, V>::get`][get]:

```rust
pub fn get<Q: ?Sized>(&self, k: &Q) -> Option<&V>
where
    K: Borrow<Q>,
    Q: Hash + Eq, 
```

So here we’re calling `get` with an `&str`, so `Q = Str`, so it needs to
be the case that `impl Borrow<str> for Run` to satisfy the `where`
constraint. And [internally, this calls `k.eq(x.0.borrow())`][i] where
(presumably) `x.0` is the map-owned key, so it boils down to checking
whether `"train" == Run("train").borrow()`.

Does this help?

[b]: https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/borrow/trait.Borrow.html#examples
[get]: https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/collections/struct.HashMap.html#method.get
[i]: https://docs.rs/hashbrown/0.9.1/src/hashbrown/map.rs.html#848-856

Assuming we’re talking about the per-document status code returned by the Index API, a Boolean flag isn’t going to be enough (there is already one of those too). The status code is used to determine how to recover from failure to index a particular document, and whether the error is retryable. The codes are documented here: https://docs.microsoft.com/rest/api/searchservice/addupdate-or-delete-documents#response

In the Track 1 .NET SDK, we took the approach of providing a default implementation of the logic that checks per-document status codes. The flow goes like this:
  1. Call Index, passing a batch of actions. 
  1. Partial failure occurs; The REST API returns 207 and a list of results, one per document, where some of them have failure status codes. Our client library takes the deserialized results, wraps them in an IndexBatchException, and throws it to the caller (the rationale for using exceptions for this case is that we don’t want customers to ignore partial failures by default).
  1. Customer code catches IndexBatchException and calls its FindFailedActionsToRetry method, passing the original batch. It returns a filtered version of that batch that includes only the failed actions that are retryable (e.g. 404 is not retryable). 

The code for FindFailedActionsToRetry is here: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/blob/bd0af1d51eaaaf8f169a3b437d1a66447e30d7a4/sdk/search/Microsoft.Azure.Search.Data/src/Customizations/IndexBatchException.cs#L49

If you still want to up-level the representation of the status code in the results object, I would suggest an object with the Python equivalent of a meaningfully-named enum, as well as the integer status code alongside it (so customers can look it up in the REST API docs if needed).

> Note the constants are updated atomically after each evaluation of a graph -- each evaluation always sees the latest set of constants, but they don't change until after the graph has been evaluated.
> 
> As I understand the spec, the result of training should be equivalent to doing this for each iteration:
> 
> 1. Load model
> 2. Run algorithm (equivalent to freezing inference graph as an operator and then running algorithm with GraphCall nodes replaced with that operator)
> 3. Update initializer values from algorithm output based on update_binding
> 4. Save model

> As soon as you've done one iteration the values of the previous iteration are inaccessible (unless you explicitly kept them, or they aren't trainable).

Yes. The only thing we need to clarify here is `what tensor to use when the user doesn't specify an optional input when calling the inference graph`. In your code, all initializers are used as `global variables`. If I invoke the inference graph twice, the same initializer may be connected to nodes in the two graphs. We can describe such two graph calls in Python:
```python
W_init = Tensor([1, 2, 3])
def inference_graph(X, W=None):
  if W == None:
    W = W_init # W is a reference to W_init
  Y = X * W
  return Y
X_1 = Tensor([-1, 0, 1])
X_2 = Tensor([2, 4, 6])
Y_1 = inference_graph(X_1)
Y_2 = inference_graph(X_2)
Z = Y_1 + Y_2
```
If we call back-propagation on `Z` agains `W_init`, the trainable tensor `W_init` may receive its gradient `dZ/dW_init`. The user might feel strange because there is no explicit connection between `Z` and `W_init`. Thus, to avoid implicit edges in the training graph, @gramalingam prefers to do this
```
W_init = Tensor([1, 2, 3])
def inference_graph(X, W):
  Y = X * W
  return Y
X_1 = Tensor([-1, 0, 1])
X_2 = Tensor([2, 4, 6])
Y_1 = inference_graph(X_1, W_init)
Y_2 = inference_graph(X_2, W_init)
Z = Y_1 + Y_2
```
Another way to avoid implicit edges is 
```
W_init = Tensor([1, 2, 3])
def inference_graph(X, W):
  if W == None:
    W = deepcopy(W_init) # W is NOT a reference to W_init
  Y = X * W
  return Y
X_1 = Tensor([-1, 0, 1])
X_2 = Tensor([2, 4, 6])
Y_1 = inference_graph(X_1, W_init)
Y_2 = inference_graph(X_2, W_init)
Z = Y_1 + Y_2
```

> ### Regarding optional inputs
> It seems the behaviour for optional inputs _within an iteration_ should look identical to the behaviour at inference time. If the input is provided then that tensor name is bound to the input value, if the input is not provided, the tensor name is bound to the constant.

The constant can be passed into optional input slot by a reference or a copy as described in my pseudo code above (by-reference case is the first snippet and the 3rd snippet is for by-copy case).

> 
> I agree that sentence is confusing:

> 
> There isn't really an edge between the `W` tensor and the `GraphCall` node when that input isn't provided -- but there is a default constant `W` which is _in_ the graph called by `GraphCall`, and that is what is used for the tensor name `W` within the graph.

Sorry for repeating my comment. Again, we don't only need to determine which value to use, we also need to determine if the value is pass-by-value or pass-by-reference.

> 
> ### Thoughts
> Some thoughts having done the exercise of writing it out:
> 
> * Should it be specified how data is consumed? Is the flow is `info[0](data[0]); info[1](data[1]); info[0](data[2]); ...`?

No. Input schema of `TrainingInfoProto.algorithm` already encodes such information. If the model author want to train the model iteration-by-iteration, the input shape could be [1, C, H, W]. If the model author thinks a batch should be used, the input shape could be [N, C, H, W]. Similarly, if the author wants to see the entire training data set, we might end up with input shape [10000000, C, H, W].

> * I'm not sure I understand/remember why having multiple training_infos is helpful, unless I misunderstood how they should be used.

It's for GAN. In GAN iterations, two parameters are alternatively updated so there are two assignments in one iteration.
```python
W = W_init
D = D_init
for i in range(10000):
  W = W - f(W, D)
  D = D - f(W, D)
```
To encode two assignments, we need two `TrainingInfoProto`.

> * As I read it, each `algorithm` can only update the inference graph and itself, so each `algorithm` would need to have its own iteration count etc. Is that what you were thinking?

Yes. It allows different stage to determine if its training is successful. In mix-precision training, the user don't need to increase iteration count if gradient is `NaN`.

No, this will never cause a problem because before allocation of pointer of `int64_t/uint32_t/uint16_t` types, we always check if it is possible to store indexes in corresponding formats. 
For instance
```
if (shape[axis] <= UINT16_MAX)
 pIdxMemory = AllocateDTypeMemory<xpu, uint16_t>(ctx, num_items);
```
The index will never be bigger than the size of corresponding dimension `shape[axis]`. 
Therefore, we should no overflow for any type of `index_t`.

Regarding the Google C ++ style guide, about not-using an unsigned integers, I'm not sure if this is a universal tip, especially when we are talking about a significant difference in runtime (and 5-10% I would consider a significant difference).

Re-defining the `function_to_call` function mimics a rule that intermittently throws exceptions - the first time we call `product_request` on the scheduler, we hit the `ValueError`-throwing version of `function_to_call` and the whole `product_request` call throws an `ExecutionError`. The second time, we redefine `function_to_call` to not throw an exception - so instead of caching the `ValueError` from the first definition of `function_to_call`, like we would have before this change, the engine re-runs the `f` `@rule`, this time successfully.

My appologies, in that case you will need tests for SPARQL `DESCRIBE` as part of the PR.

> The function **describe_cbd** is a variant of the original CBD implementation. I have modified the function to recursively consider all triples containing the query resource as an **object** and subject. The original function returns the CBD containing triples where the resource is a subject only.

If it does something different then it should at least have a different docstring which it does not, and then the next question is, why does it do something different. There are one definition of a Concise Bounded Description, and it is this:
https://www.w3.org/Submission/CBD/#definition

> However, for implementing the `DESCRIBE` query, we need all triples containing the resource – both as an object and subject. We also ensure that the blank nodes are recursively resolved – this is in line with the definition of `DESCRIBE` query.

Can you clarify why? As per the SPARQL 1.1 spec:

https://www.w3.org/TR/2013/REC-sparql11-query-20130321/#describe

> The DESCRIBE form returns a single result RDF graph containing RDF data about resources. This data is not prescribed by a SPARQL query, where the query client would need to know the structure of the RDF in the data source, but, instead, is determined by the SPARQL query processor.

And goes on to say:
> Other possible mechanisms for deciding what information to return include Concise Bounded Descriptions [[CBD](https://www.w3.org/TR/2013/REC-sparql11-query-20130321/#CBD)].

It does not state that DESCRIBE must return the CBD, but I do expect that if we return the Concise Bounded Description that we return it as defined, in which case `Graph.cbd` should do it, and if it does not should be fixed to do it.

It only really makes sense to register an abstract class if its subclasses are interchangeable when used downstream.  That's not true for `Callable`, because the arguments and return types of different `Callables` don't have to be the same.  So if you find yourself tempted to register something as general as a `Callable`, you should think about adding a more useful abstraction, or finding a different way to do what you want without adding so heavy as a new abstraction.

As for `Optimizer`, the main reason that exists is only so that we can use the type annotation in a constructor.  Doing `Registrable.register([something? "optimizer"?], ...)` defeats the entire purpose, because what do we put for the type annotation on the argument to the trainer?

I agree. In practical terms, after having had ported most of the core targets and Python targets, the only case I've found of this is wanting `resources()` to mark `sources` as required, whereas normally we want `sources` to be optional.

The vast majority of the fields have ended up either 

a) not being reused by other target types, e.g. `ZipSafe`, `EntryPoint`, `PrepArgs`, `AliasTargetRequest`
b) being reused but without any modification, e.g. `Dependencies`, `Tags`, and `Compatibility`.

The only field that has seen much subclassing is `Sources`: `PythonSources`, `PythonLibrarySources`, `PythonBinarySources`, `PythonTestsSources`, `FilesSources`, and `ResourcesSources`. Of those, the only that we could avoid if we moved this "required fields" feature from Field definitions to Target definitions is `ResourcesSources`.

I made a small change to this gsim which simply sets any NaN values in rvolc (smtk) ctx to zero. This can occur in an smtk context  where the parsed metadata does not necessarily provide rvolc due to being dependent on which ground-motion flatfile was parsed (and so data is not compiled for this distance type), but we still want to evaluate the gsim against a dataset. By setting rvolc to zero in such instances we are then able to evaluate this gsim against such datasets in the smtk (with the obvious caveat that we are not capturing the anelastic volcanic attenuation). This change is also present in the distance terms of the Zhao et al. 2016 gsim classes, and does not at all affect any calculated intensities due to rvolc in non-smtk contexts always being fixed to zero if not provided.

> Shall we allow 32-bit and 64-bit MSIs to be installed side-by-side? I don't feel this is necessary as there can be only one active az.cmd on PATH.

I don't recommend it. 1) what purpose does it serve? These are EXEs where, unlike libraries e.g., DLLs, architecture doesn't typically matter. 32- and 64-bit code can call any architecture of an EXE. It would be a UX problem because,

1) Depending on the ordering of updates to `PATH`, customer will call the first or last architecture installed. It's not deterministic.
2) Every time there's an update, the ~15-20 minute upgrade of 1 MSI will now be doubled.

And all that for what reason? Again, it's an EXE (well, effectively - an executable, more specifically) so architecture doesn't matter. SxS just unnecessarily complicates things in this case.

- Categorise changes by type (e.g. bugfix, dependency bump, addition, change etc.)
- PRs which are completely irrelevant to users should be omitted (e.g. ci fixup, docs correction)

If we use Github's autogenerated release notes we can configure both of these things by just tagging PRs: https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/releasing-projects-on-github/automatically-generated-release-notes#configuring-automatically-generated-release-notes

The code actually should be:
```
def _exclude_border(mask, footprint, exclude_border):
    """
    Remove peaks round the borders
    """
    # zero out the image borders
    for i in range(mask.ndim):
        remove = (footprint.shape[i] if footprint is not None
                  else 2 * exclude_border)
        mask[(slice(None),) * i + (slice(None, remove // 2),)] = False
        mask[(slice(None),) * i + (slice(-remove // 2, None),)] = False
    return mask
```

- name: is the name of the dataset in CARTO (also the table as you said before)
- table_name: when the query is using several tables, this prop is used to select what table do we want to update

Example: we have a query using 2 tables (table1 and table2) and we want to rename the first one to table3:
```
ds = Dataset('SELECT * FROM table1 LEFT JOIN table2 ON ...')
ds.update_dataset_info(table_name='table1' name='table3')
```

That is a worry which I also have, but there are some safe guards. Calling the Selenium options is as much as possible backwards compatible, because behind the scenes it does a similar trick as the `Create WebDriver` keyword with Python `getattr`, `setattr` and `callable`, [more details in here](https://github.com/robotframework/SeleniumLibrary/pull/1393/files#diff-566132cdf5a534d7e99598e5acc77378R388). Therefore keyword leaves to the user to correctly defined and maintain the methods and attributes user wants to call.

But there is a part that worries me and that is the importing options. First Selenium options import path may change or the options class name may change. In the past, that part has been quite stable and I have not seen any changes in the upcoming Selenium 4.0 release either. But who knows what future will bring.

Also mobile devices, which are supported on the `Open Browser` are always done by using the  `webdriver.Remote`. The remote WebDriver supports options object, but those mobile devices don't have their own opinions object (Like example Chrome or Firefox has). Therefore the keyword does not support creating options object for those mobile devices by using the string format. Also there is feature/bug when Selenium options instance object is passed directly in, for mobile devices, [it will fail](https://github.com/robotframework/SeleniumLibrary/pull/1393/files#diff-566132cdf5a534d7e99598e5acc77378R353), but that feature can be lifted away. But there is some indication that for example Android might directly support Chrome options http://chromedriver.chromium.org/getting-started/getting-started---android Also I know that some users are using SeleniumLibrary for mobile testing because, according to some users feedback: "SeleniumLibrary has better keywords but is little bit tricky to setup." Therefore it might be interesting to support also options for mobile devices, but currently I lack the knowledge how that part actually works and the Travis testing infrastructure is not not setup (Although there seems to be [support for mobile](https://docs.travis-ci.com/user/languages/android/))

In any case, if the limitation in the string format is left as it is, it should be documented. Also we should remove the limitation when the Selenium options instance is passed in for mobile devices. That limitation should be easy to change, because now we support only two formats.

With WebSocket over non-IP based transports (such as UDS, pipes, serial connections) there is no host or port at the bytes transport level. WebSocket itself however requires a fully qualified HTTP URL - and hence host+port are "needed" in as for creating the HTTP URL for the initial HTTP request that start the WebSocket opening handshake. Eg WebSocket-over-UDS, I often simply use `http://localhost` (because it really doesn't matter .. it's just to make WebSocket happy).

Because of that, I'm not sure if we want to require host and port in the ctor here .. we might make it optional, and fill in defaults automatically (host=="localhost", port==80, resource=="/")

> So in the services we'd instantiate a UserAddressManager based on the GMatrixClient and provide proper callbacks to update the routing table based on that.

Exactly.
One important caveat: In order for presence tracking to occur addresses need to be 'registered' with the `UserAddressManager` by calling the `add_address()` method. 

Question: Would it be useful for you to have an `add_addresses()` method that takes an iterable so you can add all addresses you care about after a restart in one go?

> What's the difference between address reachability and user presence though and/or which one should we prefer.

For our purposes an `address` here is basically equivalent to a Raiden node.
One node with one `address` can be represented by multiple `user`s on multiple homeservers.
In the Raiden transport we need to know about individual user presence in order to react to changes in that (e.g. invite 'new' users for already known addresses into existing communication rooms).

In the services you shouldn't need to care about users, only addresses. That means you also don't need to provide a user presence callback.

Map infact could handle concurrent requests. But the problems are how the tests are structured. For example, in `test_get_3rd_then_next_page`, you do
```python
        self.map.values(paging) # async
        paging.next_page()
```
So, you make the API call with the page value of 3 but change the page afterward without waiting for the result of `.values()`.
However, when the server sends a response for the API call, on the `get_sorted_query_result_set`  method, you access the page again. It should have been 3 but you changed it to 4. That's a problem for the first call. It may return `46, 47` or an empty list. (You don't check that but I want to say it anyway)

Now, what fails the assertion is something similar. Assume a timing like this.

-> first call is sent (empty anchor list is sent to the server)
-> second call is sent (empty anchor list is sent to server since the response of the first call is not received yet)
-> result of the first call is received (server sends the first 4 pages, you set the anchor list accordingly, now the nearest page is 2)
-> result of second call is received (server sends the first 5 pages since you sent an empty anchor list. the nearest page is 2, according to the calculations you will output the elements at 2:4 as the output which is `42, 43` but it should have been `48, 49`. (Indexes 2:4 would be correct if the second call sends something like `[(0, (41, 41)), (1, (43, 43)), (2, (45, 45))]` as the anchor list to the server) That fails the assertion and the later assertions since the predicate is in an invalid state now)

So, the problem is sharing the predicate without any synchronization for the two concurrent calls

Thoughts regarding duplicating the entire PBBIND11_MODULE macro:

* Very slightly easier to purge Python 2 support. However, even someone who's never seen this code before can purge Python 2 support from the code as-is in less than 5 minutes (just move two lines down).

* Code is read more often than it is written. Assume 100 people look at the 11 (out of 12 or 13) lines copied here. It will take most people at least a couple minutes to conclusively determine that the 11 lines are exactly identical. So the total time spent will be 200 minutes.

5 minutes << 200 minutes.
That doesn't even take into account time lost to oversights when this code needs to be modified in two places.

I feel strongly not duplicating this code is the right call here.

I don't think this is necessary at all. gevent has it's [own spawn implementation](https://github.com/gevent/gevent/blob/6d8d6fb19943ff53c75f153ee22310eb165beb9f/src/gevent/__init__.py#L93), which automatically links the [new greenlet to the hub](https://github.com/gevent/gevent/blob/6d8d6fb19943ff53c75f153ee22310eb165beb9f/src/gevent/greenlet.py#L213), and greenlets propagate the exceptions in the parent tree [ref](https://greenlet.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#parents):

> The parent greenlet is initially the one in which the greenlet was created (this can be changed at any time). The parent is where execution continues when a greenlet dies. This way, greenlets are organized in a tree. Top-level code that doesn’t run in a user-created greenlet runs in the implicit “main” greenlet, which is the root of the tree.

Meaning that if `parent` is already dead, then the exception will be raised to the hub anyways. Additionally, what does this accomplish? The hub swallows exceptions which is exactly what we don't want.

In the current implementation, failed API call's response had only two keys `success` and `message`. So, kwikapiclient raises exception using message's value in the response.

Example:
> // API call response 
> {
>        success: false
>        message: "[(xyz.com) UnknownAPIFunction: Unknown API Function: \“addd\”]"
> }
>
> // Kwikapi_client ouput
> Exception: [() UnknownAPIFunction: Unknown API Function: "addd"]

In this enhancement, we have added `error`, `code` keys in response. So, Kwikapiclient removes the success key from the dictonary and raises exception.

Example:
> // API call response 
> {
>        "code": 50002,
>        "error": "[(xyz.com) UnknownAPIFunction]",
>        "message": "Unknown API Function: \“addd\”",
>        "success": false
> }
>
> // Kwikapi_client ouput
> Exception: {'message': 'Unknown API Function: "addd"', 'error': '[(xyz.com) UnknownAPIFunction]', 'code': 50002}

The approach here is not wrong, but may have unexpected side effects.
Notice this will link erros on the spawned task with the current greenlet, which may be as far as the `_handle_response` greenlet from `GMatrixClient`. Imagine this greenlet being killed in the future (after handling of the event was already finished) because something bad happened on the transaction.
IMO, spawning and linking like that is the right thing to do (we do something similar [in matrix](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/6b913826fcc5da01fc5b2d6c73cdf1c89fb1b0fd/raiden/network/transport/matrix.py#L437-L448), but it'd be better to link to a very explicit and specific runnable/greenlet/task, instead of the current one. E.g. the `handle_transaction_event` should better link with the `AlarmTask` runnable, even if it was actually called/processed in another greenlet, `AlarmTask` is the _right one_ to die and traceback on transaction errors.
Other than that, it seems right to do it.

I'm going to think out loud a little bit to make sure we're on the same page though:

> everywhere that you create a `BitVecOperation` or `BoolOperation`, you pass in operands as a tuple without unpacking it.

For each subclass of `BitVecOperation` to correctly call its `__init__`, we need to do the following 
```python
super().__init__(size, operand1, operand2, ..., operandn, **kwargs)
```
which would pack `operand1, operand2` into `operands` as a tuple without any nesting. 

On the other hand, we shouldn't be doing this (which is what you're calling to attention, I think):
```python
super().__init__(size, tuple(operand1, operand2, ....), **kwargs)
```
right? This would double-pack the tuple if we left the `*operands` parameter as is in `BitVecOperation`s  `__init__`.

It's a similar deal with `BoolOperation`, just without the first `size` argument.

So now, we should check all subclasses of `BitVecOperation` and `BoolOperation` to see how we call the `super().__init__(...)`. 

... After some searching, I'm unable to manually find where we are packing the operands before passing to `__init__`. In that case, I'm going to add an `assert all(not isinstance(op, tuple) for op in operands)` statement to both `BitVecOperation` and `BoolOperation` `__init__` method and run the tests, which should catch any double tuple packing that happens.

I agree that I could reimplement the makedirs logic here, but it is not the design I want to follow.

The idea here is to make filesystem module as close as possible to the original os module capabilities. I do that by following a design consisting in reusing at most the existing logic in os, and change only what needs to be changed to apply the security model. 

Here the documentation says that makedirs use mkdir internally, so it is a documented entrypoint that I think suitable to use. However, I do not want to make any other assumption on how makedirs work for the several python versions that we can encounter. I prefer to consider most of the logic of makedirs as a black box, and stay consistent to the actual behavior of it in a given python version, instead of fixing a global unique logic for all of them.

One thing I like to do in test cases like these is to absolutely ensure that the function body has been executed. 

Meaning before the function (in this case the asset):

`executed = {}`

Then in the function body:

`executed["yes"] = True`

Then after job execution:

`assert executed["yes"]`

When the next engineer comes along and they see that they have high confidence that the code is actually executed. Whenever I work on a framework that is calling back into user code that is doing asserts (and not verifying outputs) I usually do this. More than once I've been bitten debugging something only realizing after some time that some function was actually never called.

Don't feel super strongly so I'm not going to block commit, but I generally find it's a good best practice.

Actually, re-reading some more SCRAM docs I think we can just get rid of this and always use `os.urandom(16)` or so .. the "hash_data" returned from the Argon2 hasher encodes the salt into it .. that is, you get strings back like `'$argon2id$v=19$m=102400,t=2,p=8$tSm+JOWigOgPZx/g44K5fQ$WDyus6py50bVFIPkjA28lQ'` which in this case has salt of `tSm+JOWigOgPZx/g44K5fQ`

That said, I don't know if it's "bad" to have a predictable salt based on email address .. maybe that still counts as "random" for the purposes of salting (after all, it's a public value), but also I'm not a cryptographer that could re-do the Argon security-analysis with that assumption instead so it certainly seems safer to use cryptographic-random salt (i.e. `os.urandom(16)`) and I don't think that has any downsides here.

It is definitely a performance/convenience tradeoff (but I would argue it is NOT an integrity one). On one hand, generation is faster and has no DB bottleneck (performant!). On the other, it does not have full 100% guarantee that the returned DOI is unique. This would cause an exception and the need to resubmit a request (inconvenient!). 

BUT :smile: 

Since `PersistentIdentifier` has a primary key on DOI value, creating a `PersistentIdentifier` with a non-unique DOI is not possible. Like mentioned above, an exception will be raised and so there is no integrity problem.

Also, although not 100%, the guarantee of uniqueness (no collision) is pretty high. For length=8, there are (2^5)^8 ~= 10^12 unique ids. A collision is something like 1/ 10^12 * #previously_generated / 10^12 i.e. in the quadrillionth chance of happening i.e. the guarantee is 99.9999999....% of uniqueness. And we can just up the suffix length if we want :)       

Anyway, the point is well taken that a guarantee would be great. If this is a tradeoff people want, I can change it as suggested. It would be interesting to hear people's thought.

This is a nice touch: now I understand why you wanted the `get_ptr_device_num` function to be visible outside of the kernels.

Since the choice of kernel changes the amount of information to be displayed (if cpu-kernels, there's nothing; if cuda-kernels, there's the device number), it should be displayed as a nested XML element, rather than XML attributes. That allows it to grow indefinitely and it makes it clear that the `on` attribute is related to the `Lib` (which shouldn't be capitalized if an attribute, anyway).

```xml
<Identities ...>
    <CUDA device_num="0" at="0x12345">
</Identities>
```

The equivalent for main memory is implicit (doesn't need to be written). This is also how Identities are included in objects, by the way:

```xml
<ListArray ...>
    <Identities ... />
</ListArray>
```

and they just don't appear if there are no identities.

This PR is only refactoring existing code by adding a common sub-class to avoid repeating the same logic in many different classes. Existing code uses `memset` already.

Here are some reasons why I would say `memset` is preferred:
- I think `Type()` does ensure zero initialization since C++11 (https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/language/zero_initialization). Note that OT still only requires C++03.
- It adds a restriction for the `Type` to have a public default constructor `Type()` so that it can inherit from `Clearable` (unnecessarily additional requirement) and that the default constructor should set all fields to zero. 
- OT is network stack and therefore is being built with a lot of older tool-chains/compliers. An older complier (not performing good optimization) may treat `*this = Type()` to create a new instance and zero initialize it and then copy it to `*this` (which adds an extra copying). `memset` approach would not rely on compiler optimization.

I can see that the main method would be more logically appropriate, but having just tried it's essentially impossible without severely bloating the main method. For each helper method call the function parameter, the model itself and the return type all have to be cast separately. Because the helper methods return tuples and only the first member of each tuple has to be cast, the return values from each helper method have to be assigned to variables before the first one can be cast. And unlike in `dropout` where the return type is bound to a parameter of the main method, the variables used for each return type would have to have different names. I think these problems are why the original code uses `#type: ignore` and find that this solution is the worse of two evils.

The current calendra already deals with some small series (Thanksgiving in US, Christmas+Boxing day in UK). I see Thanksgiving doesn't bother with shifts because it always falls on a consistent day of the week. But [here is how Boxing Day indicates that it falls after Christmas](https://github.com/jaraco/calendra/blob/fa03ff090d39e85ed2099571e14b1e8f3ec17944/calendra/core.py#L653). In this way, Boxing day knows to honor both Christmas and its shift relative to any given calendar.

Ideally, a "Series" would be handled similarly - where each day is a Holiday, and each holiday is aware of its dependencies. Even better might be to reflect the reality of the situation and let a single Holiday be observed on multiple days (and to allow that holiday to set its rules for when it's observed, relative to the configuration of the calendar).

In other words, this hook point feels like not the best place to model the behavior. I'd hope that there could be a cleaner way than keeping track of series by label and copy/pasting the observance shift from each.

@gjc13 reached out to explain the reason for moving these definitions is to allow users/projects that don't go through NCP interface (i.e. want to control through OT APIs directly) can use the same legacy APIs as in NCP case.

Thinking more about this, I feel we may not want to include these in OT directly. 
In this case (where NCP interface is not being used by the user of OT i.e., project/product integrator): 
- We are declaring a bunch of new APIs for some external module to provide.
- They are not used by core of OT.
- We expect user of OT to directly call them to use them (from the app/next layer above OT).

We are unnecessarily acting as a middle-man here. No need for OT to be aware of these APIs in this case and they can be fully defined and used by the next layer and user of OT, i.e. project/product/platform related code.

In case the user wants the code to run along with OT, we provide "extension" mechanism, but in this case, I don't think user needs to add these as extension.

Regarding NCP use, we are limited in that `npc` code it the interface for users to issue commands to OT.

Thoughts?

The Joiner Advertisement is not related to `joiner.h` much actually, though they seem to be related. Technically we can move the APIs into `joiner.h`, but I was hesitating to move these APIs to `joiner.h` for a couple reasons:

- moving user facing APIs to `joiner.h` also implies that the internal APIs should better be moved from `class Mle::DiscoverScanner `to `class MeshCoP::Joiner`
- the `AdvData` however needs sent out within `DiscoverScanner` eventually where the discovery request message will be sent out

As results of above two facts:
- the parameters (such as `AdvData` and `Oui`)need passed from `Joiner` to `DiscoverScanner`, so we'll have to either change the existing `DiscoverScanner` APIs or allowing `DiscoverScanner` to access all of `Joiner`'s the private members
- the DiscoverScanner code will have to be fragmented because `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_JOINER_ENABLE` flag will be introduced to make sure the new code only included when `MeshCoP::Joiner` code is included

And more importantly:
- doing so will disable the Joiner Advertisement function when `MeshCoP::Joiner` is not enabled, which is not always desired. E.g., the application protocol may want to use Joiner Advertisement for rendezvous purpose, but do not want to use the Thread Commissioning for provisioning.

During optimization Cerebro will try to preload (if ordered) the data in the main process -  all the data will be copied from the self.price_rows to the internal data feed buffers -  so now two copies of the day will stay in memory.  Once the optimization will start, multiple processes will be forked from the main one - effectively copying all this data unnecessarily for each process, increasing the memory pressure even more.

Suggest to support the 'preload' method and delete the self.price_row at the end of preload.

@abhinavsingh intersphinx allows you to reference objects defined on other Sphinx sites for as long as you list them here. Sphinx will download their `objects.inv` files with lists of everything exposed.
The built-in way of exploring those objects is:
```console
$ python -m sphinx.ext.intersphinx https://docs.python.org/3/objects.inv
```

But it's not very user-friendly, so I have a small project displaying this information for several sites I sometimes interact with. It's here: https://webknjaz.github.io/intersphinx-untangled/.

When linking objects from a foreign site, you can type them in as per usual, with their correct role and name. But since objects with the same name may exist on this and other sites, I prefer explicitly predending the references with `<site-alias>:`. The dict keys below are those aliases you can use to point at objects at a specific site.

* All the keys in the config (including deployment dictionaries and `ray_actor_options`) should be camel-cased.
* Some of the dictionaries need to be json-serialized:
    * `runtime_env`
    * deployment_dictionary > `user_config`
    * deployment dictionary > `ray_actor_options` > `runtime_env`
    * deployment_dictionary > `ray_actor_options` > `resources`)

*before* publishing to PyPI and NPM, then always publish from a
checked-in, known-good commit? Publishing immutable archives from a
dirty work tree doesn’t sound like something that we want to encourage.

might not be able to reliably reproduce the built artifacts. It’s much
simpler if the actual deployment process is just “clone fresh; check out
some fixed commit; run `npm publish`, et al.”.

I think we should rewrite this function, because at the end of the function there's a check and an exception if /etc/ceph doesn't exists, which is nonsense now.
I'm not sure I can commit to this branch so here goes mi proposal:

```python
def write_conf(cluster, conf, overwrite):
    """ write cluster configuration to /etc/ceph/{cluster}.conf """
    path = '/etc/ceph/{cluster}.conf'.format(cluster=cluster)

    # Stateless OS like ClearLinux does not have anything populated in /etc.
    # Create the folder before we write the config file.
    if not os.path.isdir('/etc/ceph'):
        os.makedirs('/etc/ceph', mode=0o700)

    if os.path.exists(path):
        tmp_file = tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile('w', dir='/etc/ceph', delete=False)
        err_msg = 'config file %s exists with different content; use ' \
                  '--overwrite-conf to overwrite' % path
        with open(path, 'r') as f:
            old = f.read()
            if old != conf and not overwrite:
                raise RuntimeError(err_msg)
        tmp_file.write(conf)
        tmp_file.close()
        shutil.move(tmp_file.name, path)
    else:
        with open(path, 'w') as f:
            f.write(conf)
    os.chmod(path, 0o644)
```

`confluent_kafka.Consumer.poll()` is often used in this pattern (see their own [example here](https://github.com/confluentinc/confluent-kafka-python/blob/97f08fe107d259eff6f9c281a61d92e204d2935d/examples/consumer.py#L93-L104)):
```python
while True:
    message = consumer.poll(1.0)
    if message:
        # do things
        break
```
Since consumers often poll repeatedly until they get a message, most `consumer.poll()` spans will not have a message associated with them, and the number of empty (message-wise) `poll()` spans is non-deterministic. To get around this in snapshot testing, I added a filter here to get rid of all `poll()` spans that don't have messages associated with them, so the snapshot should always test that there is a `producer.produce()` span and a `consumer.poll()` span that has a message.

i'll just think out loud in the PR :)

### step 1
the **_policy_** should subscribe to all relevant **_events_** and makes decision of the **_target worker pool state_**. and the worker pool should consume the **_target worker pool state_** and adjust the current _**workers**_ to match the target.

let's start by assuming worker can be created with zero overhead, (this implied the decision the policy made can be immediately reflected by the worker pool), and worker creation never fails.
what are the potential events that the policy might want to know to the worker pool's target state?

- worker_pool_started (implicitly, cluster runtime env)
- job submitted (implicitly, job runtime env)
- job finished
- a cached worker being leased (to run a task/actor with a runtime env)
- a new worker being created to fulfill a pending request
- a worker being returned
- a worker normally exited
- a worker unexpected exited

basically, the policy can react to each of these event, look up the workers state in the form of (WORKER_ID -> (RuntimeEnv, Resources, Leased-client), and then create or delete cached workers, and wait for next event kicks in.

### step 2
now let's relax our assumption: worker creation could take a while, and it might fail. essentially we need track additional events, and store states accordingly:

events:
- worker creation succeeded
- worker creation failed

state:
the policy need also to track additional state for each worker with an **_unique_worker_id_**, and the worker's status (PENDING, SUCCESS_CREATED, FAILURE), so that when it receives worker creation success/failure events, it can update its internal state accordingly.

With this, whenever the policy receives events, it will look up the workerpool state (unique_worker_id -> (status, runtime_env, resources, leaser...), and make a decision to start, kill or cancel pending start workers.

> The `client`, in the way you imagined it, is currently the `Processing Server` which redirects coming requests to deployed `Processor Servers` (i.e., processing worker as a standalone server - but both concepts are separated to avoid confusion).

I understand that. But the Processing Server **also** has value as a replacement for the processor CLI. It's complementary. IPs will have different models for different tasks – a full-blown Web-API backed Workflow Server with Processing Server and queues, a Processing Server without queues only Processor Servers, and finally just some scripts or custom tools with the simple CLI interface to processors. To make the latter at least more easily installable and scalable, a backwards compatible CLI interface to the Processor Servers makes sense, and would allow splitting up ocrd_all (now 36 GB!) into single module containers. The increasing problems getting ocrd_all workable in the current (fat container – few venvs) form is part of the urgency.

> Or:

> 
> Where `--address` is `Processing Server` address and the client doesn't have to bother knowing the exact address of the specific `Processor Server`. The `Processing Server` is a proxy in that case.

Yes, that's even better! So using the Processing Server as a proxy and deployer does have benefits even in this scenario.

> Of course, with proper env variables, we can also drop the `--address` option and resolve it internally.

Sorry that I didn't think about this earlier, but adding new master keys without cycling the data password also has potential security implications that can be dangerous. Think of a devops git repo where a file is readable by users A and B and contains some high-value secrets. At some point it is decided that parts of that file also need to be readable by C, so the things that should not be read by C are moved to another file, and then this command is used to give C access to this file.

Since the data key has not been cycled, C now has access to the data key **and** to the git history, allowing C to also read the parts that C was not supposed to be able to read.

This is probably why so far there's only `rotate`, which always rotates the data key.

Good catch. One pattern that we may see is the following: if we get the current mode via `os.stat(..)` first, and then modify the mode to add the executable bits - basically the equivalent to `chmod a+x /my/file`:

```
    mode = os.stat("/my/file").st_mode
    mode |= 0o111  # add executable flags
    os.fchmod("/my/file", mode)
```

I don't think we currently have this in codebase, but accepting the full format would make us a bit more forward-compatible.

But your comment helped me look into it in more detail again, actually - I did add a small change now to replace the manual bitmask `& 0o777` with [`stat.S_IMODE(..)`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stat.html#stat.S_IMODE) (this will also include the fourth bit chmod bit, used to, e.g., set the _sticky bit_).

Part of the reason my team is searching for a replacement for our current auditing system is because we need to be able to NOT create new audit records when certain field change but we WANT them included in the object serialization. Consider the following example...

We have a model for `Person` and the state of each person instance is examined every _t_ minutes for changes that may require sending the state of the person to an external system. That is, we need to sync each person with an external system every time specific conditions are met and we examine those conditions every _t_ minutes. To track this, we store a value on the `Person` instance for `last_checked_for_sync` and `last_synced`. We do NOT need to bloat our database with audit logs for each examination when the only field that changes is incrementing `last_checked_for_sync` but we do want to have that information if we look back at historical logs and examine real changes. 

I think we have some options here though. Consider that in the `serialize_kwargs` one can already add an argument for `fields` and this will be passed to the native Django serializer. Therefore if one wants to limit the fields being serialized, he/she can specify them there. If that's not intuitive enough there is another option. We could add an argument in `serialize_kwargs` for choosing fields based on the the model field selections. Something like `use_auditlog_fields_only`? On the other hand, I quite like that the current `serialize_kwargs` are all passed directly to the Django serializer though and creating our own would betray that.

If you feel strongly that we need to only be serializing fields based on what the `exclude_fields` and `include_fields` shows, then I would be reluctantly okay with flipping the script and saying that if one really needs to override that behavior then he/she would need to specify all the model fields in the `serialize_kwargs` `fields` argument. It would just be a pain for me and others in my situation.

No stupid question at all, It took me some hours to realize that the parameters `data` and `json`actually expect different formats.

 - `data` takes anything and inserts it directly into the body
```python
# fine
data=to_json(finding_description)
```
 - `json` takes anything and serializes it with the default encoder. However, the default encoder only supports the base types
```python
# would raise an exception
json=finding_description 
# in this case fine, but it will fail for nested objects (e.g. FileFindings contains
# the class Finding with the former being translated to a dict, but the member not)
json=vars(finding_description)
# fine, with method to_dict from the utils folder since any (nested) object is converted to a dict
json=to_dict(finding_description)
```

However, `to_dict(x)` is basically `json.loads(to_json(x))`.

So `data=to_json(finding_description)` is the cheapest solution, which works for any objects independent how nested thy are and `json=x` only where x is a `dict` which does not contain any custom objects.

This is somewhat related to the point I was making in my email about how we have two ways to determine when to give up the cookie: there's the capabilities that say cookie, and the RFC cookie with a domain.  Technically, in this way, once you have the cookie, you should always present it for that domain, even if the capabilities don't say to.  That's where this confusion comes in for me.

It's worth it to point out that it only uses this cookies for sessions attached to this authsession - it won't share them across anything else.  In this way, you have to be pretty explicit about which creds to use with which service.  For other auth methods, like client certificate or basic auth, there's nothing in the auth method that tells you when to use it or not, unlike cookies.  So I think in the end, we have to make a set of creds and associate them with exactly what we hope to use them with.  Even then, it won't present those creds unless the capabilities say to.

C++11 allows this model of default member variable initialization (though it is not possible for certain types, e.g., bit-fields) and we can mix and match (some variable use default initialization, some from constructor).
It is also possible to include both then the one from constructor wins:

> If a member has a default member initializer and also appears in the member initialization list in a constructor, the default member initializer is ignored for that constructor. [[link](https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/language/data_members#Member_initialization)]

In my opinion, it may be better to do all members initializations from one place (easer to see and read). Since we do need the constructor (typically to init the base class), it make sense to do all inits from the constructor.
